text.title: Spec Writing Anti-Patterns
text.summary: Six spec failures that produce untestable requirements, scope creep, or rework.

antipattern.description: Functional requirements include implementation details ("the system shall use Redis for caching").
antipattern.reason: Implementation details belong in design docs. When the implementation changes, the spec becomes wrong and requires a re-approval cycle that was never needed.

antipattern.description: Non-Functional Requirements use qualitative language: "the system shall be fast," "the system shall be secure."
antipattern.reason: Qualitative NFRs cannot be tested. Every engineer interprets "fast" differently. NFRs must include a numeric criterion (e.g., "p95 latency < 200ms", "error rate < 0.1%").
An empty Non-Goals invites every stakeholder to add scope in review. Listing what you are NOT building is the fastest way to align a room. Minimum 3 explicit items.

antipattern.description: Acceptance criteria use vague language: "the feature works correctly," "the user can complete the flow."
antipattern.reason: Vague criteria pass trivially — any implementation satisfies "works correctly." Each criterion must be a Given-When-Then scenario that a QA engineer can execute without asking a question.

antipattern.description: Open Questions section is empty or contains "none."
antipattern.reason: There are always unknowns; "none" means they were not surfaced. Hidden unknowns surface during implementation as blockers. Force a minimum of 1 question or an explicit note confirming all knowns were verified.

antipattern.description: Spec exceeds 3 pages for a single feature.
antipattern.reason: Long specs are skimmed, not read. Decision-makers approve without understanding. If a spec is over 3 pages, split into sub-features with separate specs that can be reviewed independently.
